skill.name: tdd-workflows-tdd-refactor
skill.description: Guides safe test-driven refactoring with green test baselines, incremental changes, and measurable quality checks.
skill.summary: Refactor code safely by using TDD checks before, during, and after each change.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings were reviewed against SKILL.md and appear to be false positives from Markdown formatting, prose, and example TypeScript. No prompt injection, malicious network behavior, credential access, or executable shell logic was found in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks and Code Fences
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick locations are Markdown inline code and TypeScript code fence delimiters. They do not execute commands or interpolate user input.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown syntax and not executable Ruby or shell code. No surrounding instruction converts them into command execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak cryptography alerts map to normal prose and headings, including the description, extended thinking text, and design pattern section. No cryptographic function or algorithm use is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context contains descriptive text, not crypto APIs or configuration. The alert appears to be caused by substring matching.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Keyword Matches
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance alerts map to test guidance and example validation logic. They do not collect host data, scan networks, or call external services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines mention mutation testing and an example invalid order response. There is no code or instruction for reconnaissance behavior.

content.value_statement: Large refactors can break behavior when teams move too quickly. This skill structures refactoring around green tests, small changes, recovery steps, and measurable outcomes.
actual_capabilities: Establishes a green test baseline before refactoring starts.
Identifies common code smells and maps them to refactoring techniques.
Guides incremental refactors with test runs after each change.
Applies SOLID principles and design patterns where they add clear value.
Requests before and after metrics for performance and quality comparison.
Defines a recovery protocol when tests fail during refactoring.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include executable automation scripts.
It depends on the project having useful tests or time to add them.
It does not choose language-specific tooling beyond the user context.
It cannot prove semantic equivalence without adequate tests and review.

use_cases.title: Modernize Legacy Code
use_cases.description: Plan small refactoring steps that preserve behavior while improving structure and readability.

use_cases.title: Reduce Technical Debt Safely
use_cases.description: Use tests, metrics, and recovery steps to manage risky cleanup work across a codebase.

use_cases.title: Improve Pull Request Quality
use_cases.description: Document refactoring intent, applied techniques, test results, and remaining debt for reviewers.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Safe Refactor
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a focused refactoring plan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this code with TDD safety. First establish the test baseline, then propose small steps, expected checks, and rollback points.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Code Smells
prompt_templates.scenario: Identify likely refactoring targets before changing code.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this module for code smells. Prioritize issues by risk, test coverage, and expected maintainability impact.

prompt_templates.title: Apply Design Improvements
prompt_templates.scenario: Use patterns only when they simplify the design.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this area using SOLID principles and design patterns only where they reduce complexity. Keep behavior unchanged and list each test check.

prompt_templates.title: Run an Advanced Refactor Phase
prompt_templates.scenario: Coordinate a larger refactor with metrics and recovery steps.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an incremental TDD refactor plan for this subsystem. Include baseline metrics, mutation or regression checks, performance comparisons, and a recovery protocol.

output_examples.input: A service method validates input, calculates totals, updates inventory, and sends notifications in one place.
output: Baseline tests are run before changes.
Validation, total calculation, inventory work, and notification work are separated into focused responsibilities.
Each step includes a test check and a rollback point.
The final summary lists applied refactorings and remaining debt.

output_examples.input: A team wants to remove duplicated business logic across several modules.
output: The workflow identifies duplicate behavior and confirms coverage before extraction.
Shared behavior is moved in small steps while tests remain green.
The result includes before and after notes, test status, and reviewer guidance.

best_practices: Start with a green test suite and document the baseline before editing code.
Make one behavior-preserving change at a time and run focused tests after each step.
Measure performance and coverage before claiming that a refactor improved the system.
anti_patterns: Do not combine refactoring with feature changes in the same workflow.
Do not apply design patterns when a simpler structure is easier to maintain.
Do not continue after a failing test without isolating and reverting the breaking change.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps plan and execute behavior-preserving refactors using TDD checks and incremental verification.

faq.question: Does it run tests automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and prompts the assistant to run or request the appropriate project tests.

faq.question: Can it refactor code without tests?
faq.answer: It can suggest a plan, but the safest workflow starts by adding or confirming useful tests.

faq.question: Which languages does it support?
faq.answer: The guidance is language-neutral and can be applied to most codebases with test tooling.

faq.question: How does it handle failed tests?
faq.answer: It instructs the assistant to revert the last change, identify the break, and retry with smaller steps.

faq.question: Is this only for large refactors?
faq.answer: No. It also works for small cleanup tasks where behavior must remain stable.
